title: Motor Types Compared: DC · Servo · Stepper

zones.label: DC Motor
zones.summary: Spins continuously — the simplest, cheapest motor
facts: Operating principle: electromagnetic — current through brushes rotates the armature
Control method: H-bridge for direction; PWM duty cycle for speed
Position control: none — spins freely; needs an encoder for position feedback
Rotation: continuous 360° in either direction
Typical speed: 100–30,000 RPM (depends on voltage and load)
Operating voltage: 3 V–12 V DC; cost $1–$5
MicroPython: two GPIO pins (direction) + one PWM pin via a motor-driver IC
Best for: robot wheels, fans, propellers, any continuous-rotation task

zones.label: Servo Motor
zones.summary: Holds a precise angle using PWM pulse width
facts: Operating principle: DC motor + gear reduction + potentiometer position feedback
Control method: PWM pulse width — 1 ms = 0°, 1.5 ms = 90°, 2 ms = 180°, at 50 Hz
Position control: yes — holds a target angle with ±1° precision
Rotation: 0°–180° standard; 360° continuous-rotation servos also available
Typical torque: 1.8 kg·cm (SG90) up to 15 kg·cm (MG996R)
Operating voltage: 4.8 V–6 V; cost $2–$10
MicroPython: one PWM pin — duty_u16() maps 0° → ~2000, 180° → ~8000
Best for: robot arm joints, camera pan/tilt, RC steering, door latches

zones.label: Stepper Motor
zones.summary: Moves in exact steps for precise positioning
facts: Operating principle: coils energized in sequence to rotate the shaft in precise steps
Control method: step-and-direction pulses sent to a driver IC (A4988, DRV8825)
Position control: yes — each step is a fixed angle, no encoder needed (open-loop)
Rotation: continuous; standard step = 1.8° (200 steps per revolution)
Typical torque: high and held at rest (2.0 kg·cm NEMA 14 to 40 kg·cm NEMA 23)
Operating voltage: 4 V–12 V (bipolar, coil-rated); cost $3–$15 + driver board
MicroPython: two GPIO pins (STEP, DIR) + driver IC; step by toggling the STEP pin
Best for: 3D printers, CNC machines, camera sliders, precision positioning

quiz.question: Which motor holds a precise angle like 90° using the width of a PWM pulse?
quiz.explanation: A servo reads the PWM pulse width (1 ms = 0°, 1.5 ms = 90°, 2 ms = 180°) and uses its internal potentiometer to hold that exact angle. DC and stepper motors do not work this way.

quiz.question: Which motor spins freely with no built-in way to know its position?
quiz.explanation: A plain DC motor just spins — it has no position feedback. You must add an encoder to know where it is. Servos and steppers both have built-in position control.

quiz.question: Which motor moves in exact fixed steps (1.8° each) and is used in 3D printers?
quiz.explanation: A stepper motor rotates one fixed step (typically 1.8°, or 200 steps per revolution) per pulse, giving open-loop precision without an encoder — perfect for 3D printers and CNC machines.

quiz.question: Which motor is the best and cheapest choice for driving robot wheels?
quiz.explanation: DC motors are the simplest and cheapest ($1–$5) and spin continuously in either direction, making them ideal for robot wheels, fans, and propellers.

quiz.question: Which motor needs a driver IC like the A4988 and step/direction pulses?
quiz.explanation: Stepper motors are driven by step-and-direction signals sent to a dedicated driver IC such as the A4988 or DRV8825, which energizes the coils in the correct sequence.
